herb_parts.name: leaves
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis leaves are not typically used for medicinal purposes, as the primary medicinal parts of the plant are the flowers and petals. The flowers and petals of Calendula officinalis are used to make ointments, salves, and infusions that have anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties. These medicinal properties make Calendula officinalis a popular natural remedy for wound healing, skin irritations, and other minor skin issues.

herb_parts.name: stems
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis stems are not typically used medicinally due to their low concentration of active compounds. In contrast, the flowers and leaves of calendula are more commonly used for their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. However, the stems may be used in some herbal preparations, such as infusions or decoctions, but their medicinal value is generally considered to be minimal.

herb_parts.name: pericarp
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis pericarp is not a commonly referred term in relation to the plant's medicinal properties. However, the flowers of Calendula officinalis are used medicinally and contain anthocyanins and carotenoids that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The petals of the flowers are used to create topical creams and ointments for skin conditions such as eczema and wounds.

herb_parts.name: flowers
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis flowers are rich in bioactive compounds, including triterpenoids, flavonoids, and carotenoids, which contribute to their medicinal properties. The flowers of this plant have anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic effects, making them useful in wound healing and skin care. Additionally, calendula officinalis flowers have been traditionally used to soothe skin irritations, burns, and eczema due to their soothing and protective properties.

herb_parts.name: seeds
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis seeds are not typically used for medicinal purposes, as they are often considered inedible or toxic. The petals and flowers of the plant are the primary parts used for medicinal applications, such as wound healing and skin issues. However, some herbal practitioners may use calendula seeds as an ingredient in homeopathic remedies or as a component in traditional folk medicine.

herb_parts.name: petioles
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis petioles, which are the leaf stalks that connect the leaves to the stem, contain flavonoids and phenolic acids that have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, contributing to their medicinal value. These compounds may help soothe skin irritations and reduce inflammation, making calendula petioles a popular ingredient in topical creams and ointments. The petioles of Calendula officinalis are also used to treat minor wounds and skin conditions, promoting tissue repair and regeneration.

herb_parts.name: roots
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis roots are not typically used for medicinal purposes, as the most valuable parts of the plant are its flowers and leaves. However, some studies suggest that the roots of Calendula officinalis may possess antioxidant properties. Despite this, more research is needed to fully understand the potential medicinal benefits of the roots.

herb_parts.name: style
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis style, particularly the yellow and orange petals, is used in herbal medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The style, which connects the anther to the stigma, contains bioactive compounds that contribute to the plant's medicinal value. In traditional medicine, the style is often used to soothe skin irritations and promote wound healing.

herb_parts.name: stigma
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis stigma, which is a part of the flower, is known for its medicinal properties, particularly in wound healing and skin care. The stigma of Calendula officinalis contains flavonoids and terpenoids, which contribute to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. These compounds help to soothe and calm irritated skin, making it a popular ingredient in topical creams and ointments.

herb_parts.name: calyx
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis calyx, also known as the flower bud or sepal, is a medicinal part that contains anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The calyx is used to treat various skin conditions, such as wounds, eczema, and acne, due to its ability to soothe and calm irritated skin. Additionally, the calyx has been traditionally used to treat eye irritations and conjunctivitis.

herb_parts.name: nectaries
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis nectaries, also known as the flower's sweet glands, are a rich source of medicinal compounds that contribute to the plant's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The nectaries produce and secrete a fragrant, sticky substance that contains flavonoids, triterpenoids, and carotenoids, which are responsible for the plant's therapeutic benefits. These compounds have been traditionally used to soothe skin irritations, wounds, and infections, making calendula officinalis nectaries a valuable component of herbal medicine.

herb_parts.name: pedicel
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis pedicel, which is the stalk that connects the flower to the rest of the plant, is often used in herbal medicine due to its anti-inflammatory properties. The pedicel is rich in flavonoids and terpenoids, which are known for their antioxidant and antimicrobial effects. When used in topical applications, the pedicel of Calendula officinalis is believed to promote wound healing and soothe skin irritations.

herb_parts.name: glandular hairs
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis glandular hairs, also known as trichomes, are responsible for producing and storing the plant's medicinal compounds, including sesquiterpene lactones and triterpenoids. These compounds have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, contributing to the plant's wound-healing and antimicrobial effects. The glandular hairs play a crucial role in the plant's ability to protect itself from pathogens and pests, while also providing medicinal benefits to humans.

herb_parts.name: trichomes
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis trichomes, the tiny, hair-like projections on its leaves and stems, have been found to contain high levels of flavonoids and terpenoids, which contribute to their medicinal properties. These trichomes are responsible for the plant's ability to produce a range of bioactive compounds, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial agents. The presence of trichomes on Calendula officinalis has been linked to its traditional use in wound healing and skin care applications.

herb_parts.name: volatile oils
herb_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis volatile oils contain triterpene esters, flavonoids, and sesquiterpenes, which contribute to their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. These compounds have been shown to have wound-healing and skin-soothing effects, making them beneficial for treating skin conditions such as eczema and acne. The volatile oils in calendula officinalis also exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which can help protect the skin from damage caused by free radicals.

plant_parts.name: aerial parts
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis aerial parts, which include the flowers, leaves, and stems, are a rich source of medicinal compounds. The aerial parts of calendula contain flavonoids, terpenoids, and carotenoids, which have anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. These compounds contribute to calendula's use in treating wounds, skin irritations, and infections.

plant_parts.name: flowers
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis flowers are a rich source of triterpenoid saponins, flavonoids, and carotenoids, which are known for their anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. These compounds have been traditionally used to soothe and calm the skin, making calendula a popular ingredient in wound care and skincare products. The anti-inflammatory effects of calendula flowers also make them useful in treating minor burns, cuts, and abrasions.

plant_parts.name: petals
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis petals have been traditionally used in herbal medicine for their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them effective in treating skin irritations and wounds. The flavonoids and triterpenoids present in the petals are also known to exhibit antioxidant and antiseptic properties, promoting tissue repair and reducing the risk of infection. Calendula officinalis petals can be used to create topical creams, ointments, and salves to soothe and calm irritated skin, making them a popular ingredient in natural skincare products.

plant_parts.name: volatile oils
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis volatile oils have been found to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which contribute to their wound-healing and skin-soothing effects. These oils contain compounds such as isobutyl angelate and isobutyl linoleate, which have been shown to have potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities. The presence of volatile oils in Calendula officinalis has led to its use in various topical applications for skin conditions and wound care.

plant_parts.name: leaves
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis leaves are not typically used for medicinal purposes. Instead, the petals and flowers of the plant are commonly used in herbal remedies. The petals and flowers contain sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for their medicinal properties.

plant_parts.name: flavonoids
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis flavonoids have been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them useful for wound healing and skin protection. These compounds have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria and fungi, reducing the risk of infection and promoting tissue repair. Additionally, flavonoids in Calendula officinalis may also have antioxidant effects, helping to protect against oxidative stress and cell damage.

plant_parts.name: stems
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis stems, although not as commonly used as the flowers, still hold potential medicinal properties. The stems of calendula officinalis are rich in flavonoids and other compounds that have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. These properties make calendula officinalis stems a promising ingredient in the development of natural remedies for various health conditions.

plant_parts.name: roots
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis roots are not commonly used in herbal remedies, as the more commonly used parts are the flowers and leaves. However, some traditional folk medicine practices have utilized calendula roots for their potential antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. These roots are rich in saponins and other bioactive compounds that may contribute to their medicinal effects.

plant_parts.name: glycosides
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis glycosides, specifically triterpenoid glycosides, possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which contribute to the herb's wound-healing and soothing effects. These glycosides are believed to modulate the immune response, reducing inflammation and promoting tissue repair. The glycosides in Calendula officinalis also exhibit potential antiviral and antifungal activities.

plant_parts.name: seeds
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis seeds are not typically used in herbal remedies, but they do contain some medicinal properties, particularly in traditional folk medicine. However, the more commonly used parts of the plant, such as the flowers, are rich in triterpenoid esters and carotenoids, which are responsible for their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. The seeds do contain some saponins, which may have a mild effect on the digestive system, but this is not a well-documented or widely recognized use for the plant.

plant_parts.name: tannins
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis tannins have been found to exhibit astringent properties, helping to constrict blood vessels and reduce inflammation in the skin. These tannins may also contribute to the herb's antimicrobial effects, providing protection against infections and promoting wound healing. Additionally, tannins in Calendula officinalis may help to soothe and calm irritated tissues, making it a popular ingredient in skincare products.

plant_parts.name: sepals
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis sepals, although often overlooked, contain a range of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and triterpenoids, which contribute to their medicinal properties. These compounds have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, making them a potential natural remedy for various skin conditions. The sepals of Calendula officinalis have also been traditionally used in herbal medicine to aid in wound healing and reduce inflammation.

plant_parts.name: saponins
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis saponins, though not as well-studied as other compounds, have been found to exhibit antimicrobial properties and may contribute to the plant's wound-healing effects. However, more research is needed to fully understand their role in calendula's medicinal properties. Some studies suggest that saponins may also have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, although these findings require further confirmation.

plant_parts.name: buds
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis buds contain triterpenoid saponins and sesquiterpene lactones, which have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The buds also contain flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. These bioactive compounds contribute to the medicinal properties of calendula officinalis buds, making them useful in wound healing and skin care.

plant_parts.name: phenolic acids
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis phenolic acids, which include flavonoids and phenolic acids, have been reported to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them potentially useful in wound healing and skin protection. These compounds may also contribute to the plant's antioxidant activity, helping to neutralize free radicals and protect against oxidative stress. The phenolic acids in Calendula officinalis may also exhibit anti-cancer properties, although further research is needed to fully understand their effects.

plant_parts.name: shoots
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis shoots are not typically used in herbal medicine. However, the leaves, flowers, and roots of the plant are commonly used for their medicinal properties. The flowers and roots of Calendula officinalis are often used to create topical creams and ointments to aid in wound healing and skin health.

plant_parts.name: sterols
plant_parts.desc: Calendula officinalis sterols have been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which contribute to their use in wound healing and skin care products. These sterols may also help to soothe and calm irritated skin, reducing redness and promoting tissue repair. Additionally, calendula sterols have been shown to exhibit antioxidant activity, protecting against cell damage and promoting overall skin health.
